content.text: Temperature is a scalar divisor applied to the model's logits before the softmax, controlling how peaked or flat the resulting probability distribution is. At temperature 1.0 you sample from the model's raw distribution. Below 1.0 the distribution sharpens — high-probability tokens become even more likely and low-probability ones fade — making the model more confident and less diverse. Above 1.0 the distribution flattens, giving lower-probability tokens a better chance of being selected, increasing creativity and randomness. As temperature approaches zero the model converges on greedy decoding; as it approaches infinity the distribution approaches uniform random selection.
is introduced by dividing before applying softmax:
amplifies differences between logits — if token A has logit 3.0 and token B has logit 1.0, after dividing by 0.5 the gap becomes 6.0 vs 2.0, making A even more dominant. Dividing by
compresses the gap — 3.0 vs 1.0 becomes 1.5 vs 0.5, giving B a much better chance.

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

# Suppose the model outputs these logits for the next token
# (simplified to 5 vocabulary items for illustration)
token_labels = ["Paris", "London", "Berlin", "Rome", "Madrid"]
logits = torch.tensor([4.0, 2.5, 1.8, 1.2, 0.9])

print(f"{'Token':10s}", end="")
for T in [0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0]:
    print(f"  T={T:.1f}", end="")
print()
print("-" * 56)

for i, label in enumerate(token_labels):
    print(f"{label:10s}", end="")
    for T in [0.1, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0]:
        p = F.softmax(logits / T, dim=0)[i].item()
        print(f"  {p:.3f}", end="")
    print()

# Token       T=0.1  T=0.5  T=1.0  T=1.5  T=2.0
# Paris       1.000  0.947  0.745  0.576  0.457
# London      0.000  0.044  0.166  0.211  0.220
# Berlin      0.000  0.007  0.055  0.103  0.141
# Rome        0.000  0.002  0.025  0.061  0.103
# Madrid      0.000  0.001  0.009  0.039  0.079
#
# At T=0.1: near-certain Paris (essentially greedy)
# At T=1.0: Paris likely but others have real probability
# At T=2.0: meaningful probability mass on London, Berlin, Rome
Correctness over variety; syntax errors from wrong tokens are costly
Facts don't benefit from diversity; wrong answers from random sampling are worse
Schema violations from random tokens break downstream parsing
Natural-sounding variety without incoherence; most API defaults sit here
Surprise and novelty valued; some incoherence acceptable and fixable in post
Accepts high incoherence rate; operator curates results

import anthropic

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

prompt = "Suggest a name for a new coffee shop."

# Low temperature — safe, predictable
for T in [0.0, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5]:
    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=60,
        temperature=T,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}],
    )
    print(f"T={T:.1f}: {response.content[0].text.strip()}")

# T=0.0: "The Daily Grind"
# T=0.5: "Brew & Co."
# T=1.0: "Roasted Reverie Coffee"
# T=1.5: "Wanderlust Grounds & Whimsy"

# Note: Anthropic's API accepts temperature in [0.0, 1.0].
# OpenAI accepts [0.0, 2.0].
# HuggingFace generate() accepts any positive float.
# Always check the provider's allowed range — passing T=2.0
# to Anthropic raises a validation error.

outputs probabilities that match empirical frequencies: if it assigns 80% to token A, then A should appear ~80% of the time across many samples. RLHF fine-tuning can shift calibration — models trained with strong human preference feedback often assign higher probability to their best response than the raw pretraining distribution would, making
already more peaked than in a base model. This is why instruction-tuned models work well at
where a raw base model might need

Temperature is the single most important decoding parameter to set correctly. A temperature too low makes the model repetitive and over-confident; too high makes it hallucinate and ramble. The right value is task-dependent, not model-dependent — the same model needs T=0 for SQL generation and T=1.2 for marketing copy. Always tune temperature against your actual task and measure quality with a held-out evaluation set rather than relying on defaults.

Temperature is the single parameter that determines whether a language model is useful for a given task. Set it too low and the model becomes repetitive, predictable, and formulaic — code generators loop through the same variable names, text summarizers produce the same clichéd phrases across every input. Set it too high and the model hallucinates, produces incoherent text, and fails to follow format constraints — JSON output breaks, SQL queries contain non-existent column names.

In production systems, the default temperature often mismatches the task. Most APIs default to temperatures between 0.7 and 1.0, which is appropriate for conversational chat but harmful for structured data extraction, code generation, or any task requiring deterministic output. A team deploying an LLM for legal contract analysis at temperature 1.0 may see responses that randomly vary between factually correct and slightly incorrect — acceptable for casual chat, catastrophic in a legal context. Understanding temperature is not optional; it is fundamental to deploying language models reliably.

Temperature also interacts with other sampling parameters (top-p, top-k, repetition penalty) and with the model's calibration after RLHF fine-tuning. A GPT-4 at temperature 0.7 and a LLaMA-3 at temperature 0.7 produce very different diversity levels because their underlying logit distributions differ after fine-tuning. Always evaluate temperature against your specific model and task, not from defaults designed for another use case.
: a single scalar in [0, ∞) spans the entire range from fully deterministic (greedy) to uniform random, giving fine-grained control over creativity vs. correctness tradeoff.
: temperature can be tuned independently for each task without changing model weights, making it a cheap and reversible knob for adaptation.

: generating N samples at moderate temperature (0.7–1.0) and selecting the best via a scoring function (Best-of-N sampling) consistently outperforms greedy decoding for complex tasks like math and code.
: dividing logits by a scalar before softmax adds no measurable latency or memory — the entire scaling operation is O(V) where V is the vocabulary size.
: temperature is applied before top-p and top-k filtering, so it works additively with those techniques to further shape the distribution.

: instruction-tuned models are often better calibrated at lower temperatures (0.5–0.8) than base models (which may need 1.0–1.2 for natural outputs). A temperature that works well for one model family may produce over-confident or over-random outputs for another.

: at very low temperatures, the model repeatedly samples from the same narrow distribution and can fall into repetition loops — generating "The model is very good. The model is very good." indefinitely. Combine with a repetition penalty when using T < 0.3.

: at T > 1.5, low-probability tokens get meaningful mass and the model generates plausible-sounding but factually incorrect content. This is particularly harmful in factual Q&A and code generation tasks.
: choosing the right temperature requires evaluating actual task performance, not intuition. What "feels" creative may not score well on the actual metric (BLEU, ROUGE, F1, pass@k).
: Anthropic's API accepts temperature in [0.0, 1.0]; OpenAI's in [0.0, 2.0]; HuggingFace's
accepts any positive float. Code that passes T=1.5 to an Anthropic endpoint raises a validation error.

attrs.alt: Temperature scaling pipeline: logits divided by T, softmax applied, token sampled from resulting distribution. Shows effect across T=0.1 to T=2.0 on probability concentration.

content.text: The pipeline above shows where temperature fits in autoregressive token generation. The model computes raw logits for each token in the vocabulary (step 1). These logits are divided by the temperature scalar T (step 2) — values less than 1 amplify differences, values greater than 1 compress them. The softmax converts scaled logits to a valid probability distribution (step 3). A token is sampled from this distribution using multinomial sampling (step 4), appended to the context, and the process repeats. The distribution comparison panel shows how the probability mass over 5 candidate tokens shifts from near-certain (T=0.1) to spread across all tokens (T=2.0).
Below is a complete implementation of temperature-controlled generation using the HuggingFace pipeline, including numeric stability considerations and interaction with other sampling parameters:
to enable stochastic sampling — without this, greedy decoding is used regardless of temperature.
to generate multiple diverse outputs in one call.

import torch
from transformers import AutoTokenizer, AutoModelForCausalLM

def temperature_generate(
    model_name: str,
    prompt: str,
    temperatures: list[float],
    max_new_tokens: int = 80,
    num_samples: int = 1,
) -> dict[float, list[str]]:
    """
    Generate text at multiple temperatures for comparison.
    Returns a dict mapping temperature → list of generated strings.
    """
    tokenizer = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(model_name)
    model = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(
        model_name, torch_dtype=torch.float16, device_map="auto"
    )
    model.eval()

    inputs = tokenizer(prompt, return_tensors="pt").to(model.device)
    prompt_len = inputs["input_ids"].shape[1]

    results = {}
    for T in temperatures:
        with torch.no_grad():
            if T == 0.0:
                # Greedy decoding — do_sample=False, ignore temperature
                out = model.generate(
                    **inputs,
                    max_new_tokens=max_new_tokens,
                    do_sample=False,
                    num_return_sequences=1,
                )
            else:
                out = model.generate(
                    **inputs,
                    max_new_tokens=max_new_tokens,
                    do_sample=True,
                    temperature=T,
                    # Optional: combine with nucleus sampling for safety at high T
                    top_p=0.95 if T > 1.0 else 1.0,
                    num_return_sequences=num_samples,
                    pad_token_id=tokenizer.eos_token_id,
                )

        generated = out[:, prompt_len:]  # strip prompt tokens
        decoded = [tokenizer.decode(g, skip_special_tokens=True) for g in generated]
        results[T] = decoded

    return results

# --- Usage ---
if __name__ == "__main__":
    model_name = "Qwen/Qwen2.5-0.5B-Instruct"  # small model for demo
    prompt = "The capital of France is"

    results = temperature_generate(
        model_name,
        prompt,
        temperatures=[0.0, 0.3, 0.7, 1.0, 1.5],
        max_new_tokens=30,
        num_samples=3,  # 3 samples per temperature
    )

    for T, outputs in results.items():
        print(f"
--- Temperature={T} ---")
        for i, out in enumerate(outputs, 1):
            print(f"  Sample {i}: {out.strip()}")

# Expected behavior:
# T=0.0: deterministic, always "Paris" or similar
# T=0.3: very likely "Paris" with minor variation
# T=0.7: "Paris" dominant but occasionally varied
# T=1.0: diverse city names, some plausible alternatives
# T=1.5: possibly incorrect or hallucinated cities
Temperature has a direct mathematical relationship to the entropy of the output distribution. Entropy measures the expected amount of information (surprise) in a sample from the distribution:
As T → 0, the distribution collapses to a one-hot vector over the argmax token, and entropy approaches 0. As T → ∞, the distribution approaches uniform over the vocabulary, and entropy approaches
(maximum entropy for vocabulary size V). At T=1.0, the entropy equals the model's raw entropy under its training distribution.
Entropy increases monotonically with temperature for any fixed logit vector:

This monotonic relationship means temperature is a reliable entropy knob: any increase in T increases diversity; any decrease reduces it. This is not true for top-p or top-k filtering, which can have non-monotonic effects on entropy depending on the shape of the logit distribution.

import torch
import torch.nn.functional as F

def entropy(logits: torch.Tensor, T: float) -> float:
    """Compute entropy of the temperature-scaled distribution."""
    probs = F.softmax(logits / T, dim=-1)
    log_probs = torch.log(probs + 1e-10)
    return -(probs * log_probs).sum().item()

logits = torch.tensor([4.0, 2.5, 1.8, 1.2, 0.9, 0.5, 0.3, 0.1])  # 8-token vocab

print(f"{'Temperature':>14} | {'Entropy (nats)':>14} | {'Max token prob':>14}")
print("-" * 48)
for T in [0.1, 0.3, 0.5, 1.0, 1.5, 2.0, 5.0]:
    H = entropy(logits, T)
    max_p = F.softmax(logits / T, dim=-1).max().item()
    print(f"{T:>14.1f} | {H:>14.4f} | {max_p:>14.4f}")

# Temperature  |  Entropy (nats) | Max token prob
#          0.1 |          0.0004 |         0.9996  ← near deterministic
#          0.3 |          0.1826 |         0.9362
#          0.5 |          0.6203 |         0.7448
#          1.0 |          1.4210 |         0.4610
#          1.5 |          1.7791 |         0.3319
#          2.0 |          1.9368 |         0.2600
#          5.0 |          2.0609 |         0.2061  ← approaching uniform
One of the most effective techniques for improving LLM output quality is

: generate N diverse outputs at moderate-to-high temperature and select the best using a reward model, verifier, or deterministic criterion. This approach consistently outperforms greedy decoding on tasks where the output can be verified:

: generate 10 solutions at T=0.8, verify each by executing the final numeric answer, and return the majority answer (majority voting / self-consistency). PaLM 540B with 32 samples at T=0.7 outperforms GPT-4 greedy on MATH benchmark.

: generate k code solutions at T=1.0, execute each against test cases, and return the first passing solution. AlphaCode achieved competitive performance on Codeforces problems by sampling millions of solutions at high temperature.
: generate 5 responses at T=0.9, score each with a reward model, and return the highest-scoring one. This is the basis of Constitutional AI's critique-and-revise loop.

import anthropic
import re

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def best_of_n_math(problem: str, n: int = 5, temperature: float = 0.8) -> str:
    """
    Solve a math problem using Best-of-N sampling with majority voting.
    Generates n solutions at given temperature and returns the majority answer.
    """
    solutions = []
    for _ in range(n):
        response = client.messages.create(
            model="claude-haiku-4-5",
            max_tokens=300,
            temperature=temperature,
            messages=[{
                "role": "user",
                "content": (
                    f"{problem}

"
                    "Think step by step. End your answer with 'Answer: <number>'"
                )
            }]
        )
        text = response.content[0].text
        # Extract final numeric answer
        match = re.search(r"Answer:\s*([\d,\.\-]+)", text)
        if match:
            solutions.append(match.group(1).replace(",", ""))

    if not solutions:
        return "No answer found"

    # Majority vote
    from collections import Counter
    return Counter(solutions).most_common(1)[0][0]

# Compare greedy vs. Best-of-5
problem = "If a train travels at 60 mph for 2.5 hours and then at 80 mph for 1.5 hours, what is the total distance in miles?"
expected = "240"  # 60*2.5 + 80*1.5 = 150 + 120 = 270... wait: 150+120=270

greedy = client.messages.create(
    model="claude-haiku-4-5",
    max_tokens=300,
    temperature=0.0,
    messages=[{"role": "user", "content": problem + " End with: Answer: <number>"}]
)
print(f"Greedy:    {greedy.content[0].text[-50:]}")
print(f"Best-of-5: {best_of_n_math(problem, n=5, temperature=0.8)}")

: the most frequent mistake. JSON extraction, SQL generation, YAML output, and any schema-constrained task should use temperature=0 or very close to it. Temperature=1.0 introduces random token choices that break schema compliance in a small but non-negligible fraction of calls.
Setting temperature without do_sample=True in HuggingFace

: temperature and top_p both control diversity but via different mechanisms. Temperature rescales the full distribution; top_p truncates it. Stacking both (T=1.5, top_p=0.9) does not compound the diversity effect predictably — in practice, use one at a time.
Not testing at the target temperature

: developers often prototype with a high temperature to see varied responses, then deploy at a lower temperature expecting better quality — but lower temperature can expose different failure modes (repetition, formulaic structure). Always test at the exact temperature you will use in production.

: Anthropic accepts [0.0, 1.0]; passing T=1.5 throws a validation error. OpenAI accepts [0.0, 2.0]; HuggingFace's generate() accepts any positive float. Code that runs in development against one provider may fail in production against another.

Temperature scaling is a constant-time O(V) operation on the logit vector (vocabulary size V, typically 32,000–128,000 tokens). It adds no measurable latency compared to model inference, which is dominated by the O(N²) attention computation and O(N×d_model) linear layers.
The key performance consideration around temperature is the

. At temperature 0, structured output tasks succeed or fail deterministically — failures must be handled with prompt changes, not retries. At temperature > 0, a fraction of calls produce invalid outputs, requiring retries. For a task with 5% invalid rate at T=0.7, a service running 10,000 requests/day must handle an additional 500 retries — with retry cost that multiplies with model inference cost. Always measure invalid-output rates empirically and budget for retry overhead.

For Best-of-N sampling, the total inference cost scales linearly with N. Generating 10 solutions at T=0.8 costs 10× a single greedy generation (in tokens consumed). This is often worthwhile for high-value tasks (math, code) but prohibitive for high-volume, low-value tasks (casual chat). Calculate the quality-cost tradeoff explicitly.
Values outside range raise 422 validation error
T=2.0 is equivalent to very flat distribution; rarely useful above 1.5
Must also set do_sample=True; T<1e-5 is effectively greedy
High-throughput serving; temperature is per-request parameter
Local model serving; maps to GGUF temperature parameter
Local GGUF serving; UI slider with live preview
Scale all logits by 1/T then softmax
Zero out all but top-k logits
k is sensitive to logit distribution shape
Keep tokens covering p of probability mass
Robust diversity across varying logit distributions
Can still include unlikely tokens when distribution is flat

is the most common combination for generative tasks. Use temperature alone when you need predictable diversity control; add top-p (typically 0.9–0.95) to prevent the model from sampling from the very tail of the distribution at high temperatures.

: adversarial prompts that rely on statistical improbability — producing specific banned outputs — become more effective at high temperatures where unlikely tokens gain meaningful probability mass. Production systems handling sensitive inputs should use low temperatures and output classifiers in parallel.

: applications that require reproducible outputs (legal, financial, medical) should use temperature=0. Even at T=0, some providers do not guarantee bit-exact reproducibility due to hardware differences; use seed parameters where available.

: temperature=0 enables deterministic regression tests. If you change model version or system prompt, regression tests at T=0 produce consistent outputs that can be diff'd. Tests at T > 0 require statistical comparison (e.g., 100 samples, check pass@k rate).

: high-temperature generation with Best-of-N sampling multiplies token cost by N. Implement cost tracking and per-user token limits to prevent runaway costs from users who experiment with high-N sampling in development environments.
Temperature T divides logits before softmax
: T < 1 sharpens the distribution (more deterministic); T > 1 flattens it (more random); T → 0 produces greedy decoding; T → ∞ produces uniform sampling.
Temperature increases output entropy monotonically: any increase in T increases diversity; any decrease reduces it — making temperature a reliable and predictable control knob.
: structured outputs and code generation need T ≈ 0; factual Q&A needs T ≈ 0.3; conversational chat works at T ≈ 0.7; creative writing benefits from T ≈ 1.0–1.5.
Instruction-tuned models are already biased toward confident outputs after RLHF training — they often perform well at lower temperatures (0.5–0.8) than base models (which may need 1.0).
: Anthropic allows [0.0, 1.0]; OpenAI [0.0, 2.0]; HuggingFace [any positive float]. Passing out-of-range values raises errors.
(generate N at T ≈ 0.7–1.0, select best via verifier or majority vote) consistently outperforms single greedy generation at the cost of N× inference.
Combine temperature with repetition penalty at T < 0.3 to prevent repetition loops; combine with top-p ≈ 0.9 at T > 1.0 to prevent sampling from the extreme tail of the distribution.
